name: LADDER
description: LADDER is a diagnostic benchmark for LLM-agent self-knowledge under noisy interactive feedback. It releases 37,500 session-turn JSONL records collected by running 15 frontier and open-weights LLMs through a 50-session x 50-turn protocol on 20 MMLU subjects, paired with cross-family LLM-simulated users (75% in-expertise / 46% out-of-expertise reliability). Each turn records the question, the agent's answer and confidence, ground-truth correctness, the simulated user's natural-language feedback, and an in-expertise flag. The dataset enables evaluation of per-domain capability estimation methods via the Capability Boundary Fidelity (CBF) metric, separating three competing hypotheses (H1 output channel, H2 prior metacognition, H3 noise-robust online inference) through a five-level information ladder.
citeAs: @inproceedings{ladder2026, title = {LADDER: LLM Agent Diagnostic of Domain-level Estimation Reliability}, author = {Anonymous}, booktitle = {NeurIPS 2026 Datasets and Benchmarks Track}, year = {2026}}
rai:dataCollection: Sessions were generated by programmatically prompting each LLM agent (via provider APIs or local vLLM for open-weights anchors) over 20 MMLU subjects partitioned per-model into Strong/Mid/Weak tiers using the model's own held-out validation accuracy. The simulated user is itself a cross-family LLM (Qwen-Max for Qwen/Llama/Mistral agents, GPT-5.4-mini for Claude agents, Claude-Sonnet for GPT agents) parameterised by an expertise set with empirically calibrated 75% in-expertise / 46% out-of-expertise reliabilities. Feedback decoding for L2 baselines uses an LLM-based classifier.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.rdfs:label: LADDER session generation pipeline
prov:wasGeneratedBy.rdfs:comment: All 37,500 (agent_model, session, turn) records are generated by an automated pipeline with three sub-activities described below.

prov:hadActivity.rdfs:label: Preprocessing
prov:hadActivity.rdfs:comment: For each agent_model, per-subject accuracy is computed on a disjoint 10-question/subject calibration split drawn from MMLU public answer keys. The 20 MMLU subjects are then partitioned into Strong (top tertile), Mid (middle tertile), and Weak (bottom tertile) tiers relative to that model's accuracy distribution. No human-in-the-loop preprocessing; raw API responses are stored verbatim; <0.3% of turns with API failure are logged with error reason and excluded from baseline computation rather than imputed.

prov:hadActivity.rdfs:label: Data Collection
prov:hadActivity.rdfs:comment: Collection period 2026-04-01 to 2026-05-07 (session main collection through 2026-05-05; held-out MMLU-Pro / BBH controls extended through 2026-05-07). Geographic scope: synthetic (no human subjects). For each agent_model, 50 sessions of 50 turns are sampled by programmatically prompting the LLM via provider APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, DashScope, Zhipu, DeepSeek) or local vLLM inference for open-weights anchors (Llama-3-8B, Mistral-7B, Qwen2.5-7B). Synthetic data generators: (i) the agent itself (15 commercial/open-weights LLMs listed in the README); (ii) a cross-family LLM simulated user (Qwen-Max for Qwen/Llama/Mistral agents; GPT-5.4-mini for Claude agents; Claude-Sonnet for GPT agents) routed to avoid same-family agent/user pairings, parameterised by an empirically calibrated expertise set with 75% in-expertise and 46% out-of-expertise reliabilities. Full prompts (agent system prompt, user persona generation prompt, feedback decoder prompt) are released in the code repository under code/baselines/.

prov:hadActivity.rdfs:label: Data Annotation
prov:hadActivity.rdfs:comment: Labelling schema: each turn is annotated with (correctness, confidence, in_user_expertise) where correctness is computed automatically by string-matching the agent's selected answer letter against the MMLU public answer key, confidence is reported by the agent in [0,1], and in_user_expertise is the synthetic flag determined at session-construction time. No human annotators; no inter-annotator agreement applies. Quality control: (i) 75%/46% reliability of the LLM simulated user is empirically calibrated against ground-truth correctness over a held-out 1,500-turn calibration set; (ii) the L2 baseline feedback decoder (separate LLM classifying user feedback into {wrong, correct, refuse}) is calibrated against the same set with reported macro-F1 0.91; (iii) cross-family routing controls for dual-role bias (Appendix D).

rai:dataPreprocessingProtocol: Per-model tier stratification: each model's per-subject accuracy is computed on a disjoint 10-question/subject calibration split, then 20 MMLU subjects are partitioned into Strong (top third), Mid (middle), Weak (bottom third) tiers relative to that model's accuracy distribution. No human-in-the-loop preprocessing; raw API responses are stored verbatim.
rai:dataAnnotationAnalysis: All ground-truth correctness labels are derived from MMLU public answer keys; no human annotation. The simulated user provides natural-language feedback whose correctness varies by domain (75%/46% asymmetric reliability).
rai:dataReleaseMaintenancePlan: Snapshot frozen at NeurIPS 2026 submission; semantic versioning thereafter. New models are added via PR with full session JSONL files. 6-month review cycle. Deprecated API-model rows move to an archived table with frozen scores rather than being deleted.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: None. All 'users' are LLM-simulated personas; no human subjects, no PII collected. Programmatically-generated expertise labels and persona descriptors only.
rai:dataUseCases: (1) Evaluating per-domain self-knowledge memory methods; (2) developing noise-robust second-order online inference algorithms; (3) longitudinal frontier-model calibration tracking. Unsuitable: ranking models on raw accuracy.
rai:dataLimitations: Aligned with the paper's Limitations section (§6) and Datasheet (§A.1). (1) Scope: evaluations use a 50-turn MMLU protocol with a controlled synthetic feedback channel (75% in-expertise, 46% out, cross-family routing); transfer to human-feedback channels is not directly validated. (2) Extension tasks (GSM8K, HumanEval, BFCL) use a single-shot two-step protocol because they lack the multi-domain structure CBF requires. (3) The frontier sub-sample contains 9 model endpoints; the planned next step is to expand to n~18 to refine the DS-asym vs. GLAD comparison (statistical-power calculation in Appendix A.D). (4) Use cases explicitly NOT recommended: ranking models on raw accuracy.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Positive: enables systematic evaluation of agent self-knowledge as a prerequisite for safer customer-service / financial-advisory / clinical-triage deployment. Risk: misuse as a 'self-awareness' ranking suitable for safety certification (mitigated by framing as diagnostic instrument with explicit per-tier breakdowns).
rai:dataBiases: (1) MMLU is biased toward Western/English educational content (US college subjects, English language); generalization to non-English domains is not validated. (2) The LLM-simulated user inherits biases of its underlying model (Qwen-Max, GPT-5.4-mini, Claude-Sonnet); cross-family routing mitigates dual-role bias but other biases (e.g., demographic stereotypes encoded in user persona generation) may persist. (3) Per-model tier partitioning is relative to each model's own accuracy distribution; a model with uniformly high accuracy has artificially compressed tier spread compared to a model with high variance, which CBF accounts for via the within-model normalization but cross-model magnitude comparisons should be interpreted with this in mind.
rai:machineAnnotationTools: Two classes of LLMs serve as machine annotators in the data collection pipeline: (1) the cross-family simulated user (Qwen-Max for Qwen/Llama/Mistral agents, GPT-5.4-mini for Claude agents, Claude-Sonnet for GPT agents) generates the natural-language feedback string with empirically calibrated 75% in-expertise / 46% out-of-expertise reliability; (2) an LLM-based feedback decoder (used by L2 baselines) classifies the simulated user's natural-language feedback into {wrong, correct, refuse}. Full prompts and decoder model configurations are in the released code (Appendix B of the paper).
rai:dataAnnotationProtocol: All ground-truth correctness labels are computed automatically by string-matching the agent's selected answer letter against the MMLU public answer key. The simulated user's natural-language feedback is generated by the cross-family LLM described in rai:machineAnnotationTools; its semantic correctness (whether the feedback agrees with ground truth) is recorded per turn via the in_user_expertise flag, but the feedback text itself is not human-validated.
rai:dataAnnotationPlatform: N/A. No human annotation platform was used. Ground-truth labels are derived from MMLU's public answer keys; user feedback is generated by LLM API calls (OpenAI / Anthropic / DashScope provider endpoints) without any human-in-the-loop platform.
rai:annotationsPerItem: N/A. Each (agent_model, session, turn) record has exactly one machine-generated user feedback (no multi-annotator overlap). Ground-truth correctness is deterministic from MMLU answer keys.
rai:annotatorDemographics: N/A. No human annotators were involved.
rai:dataCollectionMissingData: Rows are recorded for every (agent_model, session, turn) attempted; API failures are retried up to 3 times with exponential backoff. After all retries, <0.3% of turns may have null agent_answer / agent_confidence fields (logged with error reason); these are excluded from downstream baseline computations and are not silently imputed.

distribution.name: anon-repo
distribution.description: Anonymous repository hosting all session JSONL files, baseline code, evaluation scripts, and held-out contamination control data. The sha256 is a content identifier derived from the GitHub commit hash + dataset version (it identifies the specific snapshot rather than checksumming a single archive).

distribution.name: session-files
distribution.description: Per-model raw session JSONL files. Each file contains all 50 sessions x 50 turns for one (agent_model) combination.

distribution.name: aggregate-files
distribution.description: Per-model aggregate metric files. Contains per-baseline ECE, Brier, AUROC, HR, CBF for each agent_model.

distribution.name: heldout-n6
distribution.description: MMLU-Pro and BBH contamination-control evaluation results for the original 6 GPT/Claude frontier models. The bundled JSON contains representative per-model details for GPT-5.5 and Claude-Opus-4-7; per-category and per-task tables for the full 6-model GPT/Claude subset are reported in the paper Tables 6 and 7.

distribution.name: heldout-n3-new-frontier
distribution.description: MMLU-Pro and BBH contamination-control evaluation results for the 3 new frontier models extending the original n=6 set to n=9: DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.6-Max-Preview, and Qwen3.6-Plus. Each model is evaluated on 10 MMLU-Pro categories x 15 questions and 10 BBH tasks x 15 questions; per-category/task accuracy, ZS-SK self-estimate, and aggregate CBF are stored.

recordSet.name: session-records
recordSet.description: One record per (agent_model, session_id) pair. Contains session-level metadata and a list of 50 turn-level interactions.

field.name: session_id
field.description: Unique session identifier within an (agent_model) run.

field.name: agent_model
field.description: Model identifier of the agent under test (e.g., gpt-5.5, claude-opus-4-7, qwen-max, Mistral-7B).

field.name: user_model
field.description: Model identifier of the simulated user (cross-family routing applies).

field.name: persona
field.description: User persona descriptor (JSON object: expertise list, style, tolerance, explicitness).

field.name: n_turns
field.description: Number of interaction turns in the session (canonical value: 50).

recordSet.name: turn-records
recordSet.description: Per-turn interaction records nested inside session-records.log.

field.name: domain
field.description: MMLU subject name from which the question was drawn (one of 20 subjects per agent).

field.name: tier
field.description: Per-model tier assignment of the domain: strong, mid, or weak.

field.name: agent_answer
field.description: Agent's selected answer letter (or abstain marker).

field.name: agent_abstain
field.description: Whether the agent chose to abstain from answering.

field.name: user_response
field.description: Simulated user's natural-language feedback (variable reliability by in_user_expertise).

field.name: in_user_expertise
field.description: True iff the question's domain is in the simulated user's expertise set (controls 75% vs 46% feedback reliability).

recordSet.name: aggregate-records
recordSet.description: Per (agent_model, baseline) aggregate metrics across all sessions.

field.name: baseline
field.description: Baseline name (key under aggregate object). One of: NoMemory, ZeroShot-SK, LLMSelfAssess, UF-PDC, Platt-Online, HistBin-Online, Bayes-Domain, EM-Joint, OraclePDC, GT-SelfStats, DS-asym, GLAD.

field.name: CBF
field.description: Capability Boundary Fidelity, the central metric.

field.name: HR
field.description: Hallucination Rate: fraction of high-confidence wrong answers.
